---
title: Ex.1 Parsing Protein Structures
format: html
Authors: "Marta Aransay, Carlota de la Concepción, Marta Rodrigo, Lucía Santos"
date: 04/03/2026
---

# Introduction

## Objective


---

# Methods

## Data Collection
[Describir que los hemos sacado de lo que nos ha dado el profesor pero añadir algo más sobre las proteínas, de donde salen, info principal de la página de PDB, etc.]

## Parsing PDB Files
[Explicar el objetivo de esta función y describir las partes más importantes del código. Incluso por simplificar se puede meter una tabla con metadato -> de donde se extrae -> parte del código que lo hace]

The data base PDB is one of the most widely used worldwide in the field of bioinformatics, among other aspects for its accessibility and the large amount of information it contains. Taking advantage of the possibility of querying through its API, we have been able to extract the necessary data through a parsing function *parse_pdb*.

The objective of this function is to parse the PDB files of the studied proteins, extracting the relevant information for subsequent analysis. Specifically, we have obtained the descriptive and experimental information associated with each entry of the Protein Data Bank.

The function defined as *parse_pdb* performs two independent queries to the RCSB PDB REST API, one at the structural level (core/entry) and another at the polymer entity level (polymer_entity). This separation allows us to access both the experimental details of the protein and the specific information related to it. 

In first place, the function implements a basic system of error handling to ensure that the queries are successful. It handles errors due to timeouts and inexistent entries (HTTP status code 404).

Once the queries are successful, the function extracts the relevant information from the JSON responses:
- **PDB ID**: Unique identifier for the protein structure.
- **Protein Name**: The name of the protein.
- **Source Organism**: The organism from which the protein was derived.
- **Determination Method**: The method used to determine the protein structure (e.g., X-ray crystallography, NMR spectroscopy).
- **Authors**: The researchers who contributed to the determination of the structure.
- **Resolution**: The resolution of the structure, if applicable.
- **Number of Peptide Chains**: The number of peptide chains present in the structure.
- **Number of Other Molecules**: The number of other molecules (e.g., ligands, water) present in the structure.

There are also some fields with multiple values, such as authors, experimental methods, etc. For those cases, an auxiliary function *extract_join_metadata* is used to concatenate the elements of the list into a single string, separating the values with commas. This facilitates the presentation of the information and its subsequent analysis.

All this information is organized into a structured fromat, as it is a dictionary, which allows its conversion into a pandas DataFrame and its export to a CSV file for further analysis and visualization. 

In case of any error during the queries, None is returned. 



## Metadata Extraction

## Distance and Contact Map Calculations

[Primero hacer una introduccion a que es la distancia el mapa de contacto (por qué son útiles), meter alguna explicacion matemática, y luego lo mismo que antes, describir las partes más importantes del código.]

\[
d_{ij}^2 = (x_i - x_j)^2 + (y_i - y_j)^2 + (z_i - z_j)^2
\]

---

# Results

TABLA CON LOS RESULTADOS DE LAS DISTANCIAS Y CONTACTOS

---

# Discussion

---

# Conclusion

---

# Annexes

## Work Register

[Aquí básicamente pondría el paso a paso que hemos seguido en github, con los commits, etc. para que se vea el proceso de trabajo]
